title: Building informative materials datasets beyond targeted objectives
authors: Rafael Espinosa Castañeda, Ashley Dale, Hongchen Wang, Yonatan Kurniawan, Hao Wan, Runze Zhang, Adji Bousso Dieng, Kangming Li, Jason Hattrick-Simpers
abstract: Materials science data collection can be expensive, making the reuse and long-term utility of datasets critical important for future discovery campaigns. In practice, researchers prioritize a subset of properties due to research interests. However, ignoring a subset of outcomes in data collection campaigns potentially generate datasets poorly suited for future learning tasks. Here, we present a framework for dataset construction that maximizes informativeness for target properties of interest while preserving performance on untargeted ones. Our approach uses diversity-aware selection to ensure broad coverage of the materials space. In noisy experimental dataset construction, we find that without our diversity-aware framework, prediction performance on untargeted properties can degrade by up to 40% relative to random sampling, whereas applying our framework yields improvements of up to 10% . For targeted properties, performance can degrade with respect to random sampling by up to 12.5% without diversity, while our framework achieves gains of up to 25%. Incorporating diversity into dataset construction not only preserves informativeness for the targeted properties, but also improves materials coverage for potential future objectives. As a result, the constructed datasets remain broadly informative across considered and unconsidered outcomes, ensuring unbiased quality entries and mitigating cold-start limitations in subsequent modeling and discovery campaigns.
summary: This paper proposes a new method for building materials datasets that balances immediate research goals with long-term utility. By incorporating feature-space diversity into the selection process, the framework prevents the sampling bias that typically makes datasets useless for predicting properties not explicitly targeted during collection.
main_problem: Materials science data collection often focuses on specific target properties, creating sampling bias that reduces the utility of datasets for future, unanticipated scientific tasks.
main_result: A diversity-aware framework improves predictive performance on untargeted properties by up to 10% and prevents the 40% performance degradation seen in purely targeted strategies, especially in noisy experimental datasets.
method: The authors use a multi-objective optimization approach with the NSGA-II genetic algorithm to balance target property informativeness with feature-space diversity (using cosine dissimilarity).
model_or_system: The study evaluates the framework using DFT-based repositories (Materials Project, JARVIS) and the experimental sysTEm thermoelectric dataset, utilizing structural and compositional descriptors via Voronoi tessellation.
key_observables: Electronic bandgap, elastic bulk modulus, formation energy, thermoelectric figure of merit (zT), thermal conductivity, electrical conductivity, and Seebeck coefficient.
important_parameters: High-dimensional feature space (up to 273 dimensions), iterative selection of 1% of the pool per iteration, and weighting between informativeness and diversity.
main_assumptions: Assumes that chosen properties are weakly linearly correlated to ensure true cross-objective generalization; assumes the utility of cosine similarity for diversity in high-dimensional spaces.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the data selection pipeline, RMSE curves for various properties, and comparisons of chemical manifold coverage (PCA space) between targeted and diversity-aware sampling.
paper_structure: Introduction of the sampling bias problem, description of the diversity-aware framework and NSGA-II implementation, evaluation on DFT and experimental datasets, and a detailed appendix of performance metrics across various property combinations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of data selection pipeline for each iteration when explicitly incorporating diversity in feature space. The image shows how 1% of the pool data is selected. For each iteration, performance of the two models-Random Forest and XGBoost on hold-out test dataset is recorded. The pipeline is repeated until the complete pool data has been selected.
Figure 2: RMSE curves on hold out test data for bandgap, bulk modulus and formation energy when the target used for data construction is bandgap using as pool JARVIS18.

Figure 3: Data Manifold coverage comparison without and with diversity for single target DFT dataset building. Notoriously, skewed distributions are obtained even with 50% of sampled data when unconsidering diversity.
Figure 4: Random Forest improvement of all policies respect to random sampling with single outcome targeting in DFT datasets construction.
Figure 5: Random Forest improvement of all policies respect to random sampling with single outcome targeting in experimental datasets construction. Using as pool the sysTEm dataset.
